verdict_reasoning: HSPD1/HSP60 is primarily a mitochondrial matrix chaperonin with no transmembrane domain or GPI anchor. However, extensive literature documents its presence on the outer face of the plasma membrane under specific cell states. Surface HSP60 is found associated with lipid rafts on tumor cells (FACS with anti-Hsp60 antibodies confirms N- and C-termini are extracellularly accessible), and its levels at the cell surface increase during inflammatory states and tumorigenesis. The mechanism of translocation is non-conventional (lipid raft/exosome pathway, or in malignant cells the ER-Golgi secretory pathway), and surface expression is restricted to stressed, inflamed, or malignant cell states — not constitutive across all cell types. The surface pool, while a minority fraction (~10% of cells constitutively in some lines), is wash-resistant enough to be detected by intact-cell biotinylation and FACS. This qualifies as cell_state_induced contextual surface accessibility.
